data.title: A Jobs Bloodbath Affirms Our Down View Of The Economy
data.description:
NEW YORK (March 6) - The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) today reported 92,000 jobs lost, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey.

The number was a catastrophic miss from the 65,000 jobs that markets had anticipated. The private payroll number showed 86,000 jobs lost after taking account of 6,000 government jobs lost. December and January revisions resulted in an additional 69,000 jobs lost.

For the three months from December, 2025, average monthly jobs creation has been less than 6,000 jobs per month, affirming our view in prior jobs and GDP reports that the economy is slowing.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed even worse than the Establishment Survey, showing 185,000 jobs lost.

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate were 4.4% and 7.9%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Just 18,000 people joined the civilian labor force, the Labor Force Participation Rate fell nonetheless, to just 62%.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of November and December Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

As you can see, jobs data for February was a veritable blood bath, with virtually no bright spots in the current months' (green) bars. So let’s move on to the status of the…

Economy Generally

The onset of the Iran War has significantly altered the outlook for the economy. Gasoline and diesel fuel prices have increased significantly, according to this chart adopted from AAA:

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) have also spiked to their highest level in a decade, as seen in the SA Chart linked here.

Those higher fuel prices will be felt throughout the economy.

Though there is some debate, it is generally observed that spikes in fuel prices tend to be disinflationary, and even deflationary, at least in the government statistics roundup because the Fed’s reported preferred measure of inflation, Core PCE inflation, excludes food and fuel. Since consumers’ disposable income will likely be eaten up by higher fuel prices, less is left for them to spend on consumer staples and discretionaries. (We already see some of this in the disposable personal income figures where spending has exceeded disposable income, in the "Other Data Points" section of this report.)

The market has largely gone “risk:off” because of the war. The global economy has been negatively affected as well, as Iran continues to threaten transshipments of oil from the Persian Gulf by bottling up the Strait of Hormuz. It’s being reported today that Kuwait has ceased production because its oil storage facilities are full. Iraq has cut production as well, and Qatar is threatening the same.

One-fifth of the world’s oil and natural gas supply has been cut off and the cash flow to the aforementioned countries has been cut off. (See the important "GEOPOLITICAL NOTE" below, as it could substantially affect the economy.)

The economic consequences of the war highlights that the Fed has botched rates for several meetings and that private, business-oriented hiring - outside the healthcare and education sector - has slowed considerably.

We note, too, that the job opening rate, per the Beveridge Curve, is 3.9%, the lowest it has been since May, 2017.

The “green shoots” we had hoped to see coming in the latter quarters of this year from capex from re-shoring may have dissipated because of the Supreme Court’s tariff decision. We may still see significant gains in the construction sector coming from AI data center construction, but local opposition to such builds will delay job growth from them into later quarters or years.. That said, we note that the ISM Manufacturing Index, discussed and linked below, is in its second month of “growth” after a near year of "contraction.

Average jobs creation over the last three months has been a paltry 6,000 jobs per month.

Core CPE inflation has stepped up somewhat in December, to 3.0%. Part of that has been rough winter weather in population centers in the Northeast and northern Central States; some has occurred because of major healthcare worker strikes. The Labor Force Participation Rate was just 62%.

It should not be lost on the Fed that the labor force participation rate is likely to increase in 2026 due to work requirements for SNAP recipients. (Work requirements for Medicaid start in 2027.)

While the additional workers might be absorbed from the generous fiscal policy of the “Big Beautiful Bill,” high interest rates will inhibit the ability of small and medium sized businesses - the traditional engines of job growth - to access the capital that will allow them to make use of first year depreciation, lower hiring costs (from tax free tips), etc.

The Fed's discount rate should probably be about 2.25 to 2.75%, about 100 bps lower than present. Even if it was that low, the discount rate has little effect on longer-term rates, which are the problem for jobs creation.

We reiterate our desire to see the incoming Fed chair, Kevin Warsh, engage in yield curve control so that longer-term rates can be reduced. Alternatively (or, perhaps, in conjunction with that monetary policy), we would like to see Congress enact fiscal policy that would exempt Treasurys entirely or partially from Federal Income Tax. Yield curve control sets longer term rates within thresholds set by the Fed, with the Fed buying and selling to keep such rates within the targeted range. Exempting Treasury interest from Federal Income Tax would increase demand, raise bond prices, and thereby lower rates. In addition, since rates would decrease, it would help ameliorate some of the deficit since the interest on the federal debt could be reduced. (We did this during World War I to control rates and inflation, so it’s not unprecedented. It would, however, obviously “horn in” on the municipal bond market, so that rates there would increase and cause higher yields since they would not be as attractive as the federal bonds.)

PROGNOSTICATIONS

We reiterate our view of the last several months that the data point to a slowing economy. PCE spending is slowing; however, we think there may be substantially increased GDI owing to the Big Beautiful Bill, but that won’t be reflected, likely, until at least 2026Q2 or Q3. That would make the mid-term elections a jump ball.

The Trump Administration needs to better manage expectations if it expects to maintain Congress in November. Otherwise, given the mood of the Democrat caucus, it’s almost certain the president would, again, be impeached and his agenda scuttled.

The president also needs to message his war aims and casus belli far better than he has so far. This morning, we learned that the war aim is “unconditional surrender”.

But that objective should have come in a national address, not a “Truth” post. Such an address would have had the president explain how unconditional surrender will be achieved without U.S. ground troops or an air campaign that does not include nuclear weapons.

I cannot think of a historical instance where “unconditional surrender” - or even regime change - has been effected solely in an air campaign. (Kosovo, possibly? In Japan, we used two nuclear weapons and we still left Hirohito in place, despite his war crimes.)

In view of today’s release, we are downgrading our estimate of 2026 Q1 to be 1.25%, +/- 25 bps. We expect the same for 2026Q2 for now, but it will depend on the length of the war. We expect the Fed will stand pat with interest rates at its March meeting.

Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at 1.4% in 2025Q4, according to the Advance Estimate released February 20th. Core GDP, or real final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at 2.4%.

The personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index increased 2.9 percent. Excluding food and energy prices, the PCE price index increased 3.0 percent. Core PCE inflation is, reportedly, the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturer's Purchasing Managers Index (PMI), for February, released Monday, fell slightly to 52.4 from 52.6. This is the second month of a growth trend from an prior 11 month contraction. The February Services Index, released Wednesday, printed at 56.1, up 2.3 points from January.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey, JOLTS, for December, was released February 5th with 386,000 fewer job openings than in November.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in December were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,448,000. This is 4.3 percent above the revised November rate of 1,388,000, but is 2.2 percent below the December 2024 rate of 1,480,000. Privately-owned housing starts in December were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,404,000. This is 6.2 percent (±10.7 percent)* above the revised November estimate of 1,322,000, but is 7.3 percent (±14.0 percent)* below the December 2024 rate of 1,514,000.

Personal income increased $86.2 billion (0.3 percent at a monthly rate) in December, according to estimates released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $75.7 billion (0.3 percent), and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $91.0 billion (0.4 percent).

RealClearMarkets/TIPP Economic Optimism Index for March printed at 47.5, continuing a down trend..

GEOPOLITICAL NOTE: The reduction in cash flow and employment in the oil producing countries affected by the war could have adverse geopolitical consequences in some of those countries that could have a catastrophic affect on the world economy. We all remember the populist uprisings of the Arab Spring. Oil money has largely pacified the lower-income, less-educated, populations of the oil producing countries, who are the overwhelming majority of their populations. There is at least some risk of popular uprisings in those countries should the oil shut-down last terribly long. Such uprisings might also be triggered more imminently over water if Iran hits the many desalination plants that serve these desert areas. We should not underestimate the strategic value to China of inciting such anti-government movements among the oil-producing countries, several of which are US allies and hosts to forward US military bases.

______________________________________________

AUTHOR'S NOTE: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change.)

data.title: Carrefour: Like Walmart, But With A 7% Dividend Yield
data.description:
Yesterday, I was looking at some Walmart (WMT) charts and saw this bizarre thing:

Today, Walmart's forward P/E ratio is 43x, which is already high considering its average of 27x. But there's a huge gap when we consider the Nasdaq (QQQ), and this shows how the market is (heavily) rewarding some stocks.

But then I had the idea of checking out Walmart's global peers. I know that domestic peers, such as Costco (COST), are just as expensive or even more expensive, and basically that's the only player as big as Walmart. But it turns out that some names like Carrefour SA (CRRFY) (CRERF) aren't just a little cheaper; they're much cheaper than Walmart, which shows how the market is also neglecting some regions.

We aren't looking at a fragile player in the supermarket industry or, for instance, a big player in a volatile region such as Latin America. Carrefour is actually a European behemoth, but with a very low valuation (and with a dividend yield of 7.4%). I'm starting my coverage of Carrefour with a buy.

Carrefour Isn't A Small Company

I think that before delving into Carrefour's risks, it's worth looking at its big numbers, precisely because the single-digit P/E makes it look like it's a dying company or one with very high risks, which definitely isn’t the case.

Carrefour has about 15,000 stores, and check the image below; they're a leader or second in core regions. They're a leader only in Brazil, and the market is still very fragmented, so it has a lot of capacity for expansion. Last year, Carrefour had €91B+ in revenue. Of course, it's still far from Walmart's size (around 87% lower), but it's still a big figure, especially in the European context.

Still comparing it with Walmart, Carrefour doesn’t have a giant ecosystem yet, but we can't say it's just a simple supermarket. Private labels already have very high penetration, which helps with margins, and e-commerce is in a good momentum, not to mention portfolio optimization moves such as the delisting of Brazilian Carrefour.

This wouldn't be that appealing if Carrefour's bottom line (mainly cash flow) weren't already quite solid. In 2025, EBITDA was €4.5B, with very low growth of around just a little higher than 3% (ignoring the FX impact). The top line was kind of similar, growing a little lower than that on a like-for-like basis (they call it LFL, but it's similar to comparable sales or same-store sales).

It may not seem like much growth, and it really isn't, but in terms of cash flow, it becomes more interesting. Net free cash flow, after expansion capex and everything else, Carrefour left €1.3B in free cash flow, which for a company worth around €10 billion is already an absurd FCF yield. What impresses me most is that Carrefour's management wants to achieve around €5B accumulated net free cash flow (I will call it NFCF or FCF from now on) in the next three years, basically three years of FCF, which would already pay half of the entire Carrefour's market cap.

Carrefour also isn't a high-leverage company; the net debt/adjusted EBITDA is lower than 1x.

Carrefour Stock Is Dirt Cheap

Taking the figures I just mentioned, it's quite clear that Carrefour's case is very appealing in terms of valuation. It's a cash cow, has huge free cash flow linked to real assets, with some inflation protection because it focuses on low prices and has scale (kind of like Walmart), and still manages to offer a free cash flow yield of 12%. If we look at Walmart's operating cash flow yield (yes, I'm not even taking free cash flow), it's already close to 4%.

If you take other multiples, it's also a very large distortion. The forward P/E is 8.5x, while WMT's is 38x and Costco's is 45x. The EV-to-EBITDA is less than 6x, and for the two American behemoths, it's more than 20x.

Besides, we're also talking about a deep value case: Carrefour has 14 billion in real estate, with ~9 billion in France and 3 billion in Brazil. For this reason, Carrefour's P/B ratio is less than 1x. And this real estate has been a very interesting asset in recent years, as management highlighted in the recent call:

“It comes out that the value of our real estate portfolio for France, Spain and Brazil amounts to EUR 14.2 billion at the end of 2025. Over the past four years, the value of this portfolio has grown by EUR 2.8 billion or plus 25%.”

I'm not saying that Carrefour doesn't deserve a discount or that Walmart doesn't deserve a premium, but this gap is too big. If Carrefour traded at a P/E of 12x and Walmart at a P/E of 25x, it would seem quite reasonable to me. But Carrefour certainly doesn't seem so bad to me that it should be offering a dividend yield of 7%, a double-digit free cash flow yield, and everything else.

Risks And Bottom Line

I have to mention that I really don't expect a re-rating on Carrefour stock; it won't trade for the same multiples as WMT stock. There are some points that you will notice about Carrefour's case that make it less comfortable. As it operates in several geographies at the same time, the tax rate is much higher, with the normative rate in 2025 being 27.9% and the effective rate being 33%.

Then we have the Brazil "problem." It's not a real problem, but about 20% of sales are in the Brazilian operations, and we're talking about a country with a volatile environment, inflation, high-interest rates, and other matters. That's why debt is not a concern, but Carrefour's interest rates are quite high. Net financial expenses in 2025 were €578 million.

And in this geographical mix alone, which is closely related to inflation, interest rates, and regulation, Walmart already has a significant advantage. Mainly because we must remember that in France, there's a strong issue related to unions, regulation, and even certain political pressure regarding price increases. Also, the demographic dynamics in Europe compared to the US are not as favorable.

So it really is a more complex and riskier case, not risky to the point of being disrupted, but to the point where the scenario is more nebulous - perhaps more difficult to grow; perhaps the portfolio optimizations will take a little longer to have a bottom-line benefit, and this uncertainty needs to be considered when buying the stock.

But still, as it's a very resilient company with a strong brand, a large geographical presence, real assets (and real estate), and a very undervalued valuation, my rating is a buy for Carrefour stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy Oracle's 'Bubble Fears' Before Next Week's Q3 Earnings Report
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Oracle (ORCL) stock may have finally struck a bottom.

The stock has been struggling since September 2025, and its miseries did not end in 2026 either, as the stock fell another ~20% on a YTD basis.

This was driven by investor sentiment souring on AI capex as the four other hyperscalers that include Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) significantly raised their capex projections for FY26 in their latest earnings reports. The only thing that was different this time was that these companies would also take a severe hit to their free cash flow and increasingly tap into the debt markets, similar to Oracle to fund their capex goals, raising AI bubble fears.

As a result, we have seen investors abandon the hyperscaler trade in 2026.

Up until now.

Over the last week or so, these companies are once again outperforming the S&P 500, even as geopolitical uncertainty worsens.

The way I see it, this could be tied to OpenAI's $110B funding announcement from two weeks ago, which gives investors more confidence that it can fulfill its $250B compute contract with Microsoft and $300B compute contract with Oracle.

Not only that, but even Nvidia’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) Jensen Huang also firmly pointed out that $3-$5T of capex buildout over the next several years is necessary for every data center to transition to accelerated computing that would require them to handle the exponential growth in tokenization as we enter the Agentic AI era.

So, while the revenue materialization risks still remain, along with the power constraint introducing a physical ceiling on how fast the revenue gap can close, investors have been rotating back into hyperscalers once again, including Oracle, which is up 9.5% over the last five days, outperforming the other four.

The company is slated to report its Q3 FY26 earnings on March 10, when revenue is expected to accelerate to 19.17%, with no signs of a slowdown after that until FY29. With the company taking certain measures to stabilize its CDS (Credit Default Swap) spreads along with its layoff announcement yesterday to boost profitability, I will reiterate my “buy” rating at current levels.

A Quick Recap Of What I Said Earlier

In my previous post on Oracle, I pointed out three things from Oracle’s Q2 FY26 earnings that fed the AI bears exactly what they wanted to hear.

First, its Cloud Revenue (Infrastructure + Applications) revenue missed analyst estimates for the second consecutive quarter.

Second, the earnings per share beat was largely attributed to a one-time pre-tax gain of $2.7B from the sale of its interest in the Ampere chip company.

Third, Oracle’s capex came in $3.7B higher than what analysts were expecting at $12B. On top of that, Oracle management further raised their full-year capex to $50B, up from their previous estimate of $35B, which would now amount to 75% of total revenue, which is significantly higher than the other four hyperscalers that include Microsoft, Meta, Google, and Amazon, even after these companies have substantially raised their full-year FY26 capex projections as well as of the latest quarter.

Therefore, despite Oracle demonstrating 15% QoQ growth in backlog, investors chose to focus on their free cash flow, which had gone from a positive $11.2B to a negative -$13.2B in just six quarters, suggesting a lot of debt-fueled capex ahead, along with customer concentration risk from OpenAI, accounting for 60% of Oracle’s backlog.

During the previous quarter, Oracle increased their long-term debt from $82B to over $99B. Given their significantly higher debt-to-EBITDA ratio compared to that of other hyperscalers, their CDS spread is also much wider, reflecting investor concerns on ROI on AI capex.

The AI Capex Narrative Came Under A Significant Pressure Test In 2026.

Since Oracle’s earnings at the end of 2025, we have heard from Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, which have all significantly increased their FY26 capex to grow roughly 70%, 97%, 52%, and 73% YoY, respectively.

In other words, we are now looking at a combined $680B in AI capex for 2026 for the four companies above (excluding Oracle), which is expected to be approximately double that of the FY25 figure.

Yet, despite these companies beating their revenue and earnings estimates in the latest quarter, all four are down for the year, underperforming the S&P 500.

As this X thread points out, this is all about how AI capex is altering the free cash flow mechanics at these companies, and investors are not too happy about it. Here’s how the author framed the math below.

“The math: last year the four hyperscalers generated $200 billion in combined free cash flow. This year they’re guiding to nearly $700 billion in capex. Amazon alone expects $200 billion in spending, and Morgan Stanley projects its free cash flow goes negative by $17 billion. Alphabet’s FCF is forecast to drop ~90%, from $73.3 billion to $8.2 billion. Alphabet did a $25 billion bond sale in November, then another $20 billion sale in February. Their long-term debt quadrupled in 2025 to $46.5 billion.”

In other words, these companies that once used to fund their AI capex using their own free cash flow are now expected to tap into the debt market as revenue fails to realize fast enough in the short term.

Meanwhile, Michael Burry has also been mass publishing on the depreciation cliff that these companies face and that the markets haven’t priced in yet. According to him, reported operating income may be inflated by as much as 20% at companies like Oracle and Meta given the accounting gap between Nvidia’s GPU architecture cycles and their depreciation timelines.

Not only that, assuming a 20% annual depreciation on $2T in estimated capex by 2030 across these five hyperscalers, it amounts to $400B per year, which exceeds the combined profits these companies generated in 2025.

Aside from the revenue materialization risks that these companies face today, a power constraint also introduces a physical ceiling on how fast the revenue gap can close. According to this post written by Robert P. Ballan, power generation buildout takes 3-7 years, which is much longer than chip manufacturing cycles. In other words, there is a probability that we run into a scenario whereby the temporal mismatch could translate into "billions of dollars of the most advanced AI hardware ever built may sit dark — depreciating on balance sheets, servicing debt, generating zero revenue — while waiting for power infrastructure that is years behind.”

Given the overarching risks facing the future of free cash flow generation at these companies, investors abandoned the hyperscaler trade altogether in 2026, as you can see in their YTD performance below.

A Sigh Of Relief After OpenAI’s $110B Funding Round

However, we may be finally at an inflection point in the AI capex narrative that turned south at the start of the year.

It started with Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang defending the $3-4T of AI capex buildout over the next several years, as he believes it to be necessary for every data center to transition to accelerated computing to handle the exponential growth in tokenization as we enter the Agentic AI era.

“In this new world of AI, compute equals revenues. Without compute, there’s no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there’s no way to grow revenues. [...] I am certain at this point that we’ve reached the inflection point.”

As this X thread points out, this isn’t discretionary capex that the hyperscalers are investing in. This is survival capex as we enter a regime where the companies that deliver the lowest cost per token per watt win.

Furthermore, what may have also helped improve investor sentiment on the AI capex narrative could be OpenAI’s announcement of an $110B funding round on February 27, which is more than double the size of its last raise a year ago.

A week prior to that, OpenAI bumped its 5-year revenue forecast up by 27%, targeting $280B in revenue by 2030 as they hit a new peak of 910M Weekly Active Users, with GPT 5.1 and 5.2 updates re-sparking growth. This would mean that OpenAI would now need to grow its revenues by 70% CAGR.

To this, BNP Paribas analyst Stefan Slowinski pointed out in a note to clients that both Microsoft and Oracle should benefit from OpenAI’s latest funding round as the company is now well positioned to fund a $250B compute contract with Microsoft and a $300B compute contract with Oracle, which I agree with.

Is The Downside Risk Fully Priced In?

Oracle is scheduled to report its Q3 FY26 earnings next week on March 10, where revenue is expected to grow at an accelerated rate of 19.67%, followed by no signs of slowdown at least until FY29.

To that, the good news is that despite the tremendous downward volatility in the stock price, there have been no downward revisions to the company’s forward revenue growth estimates.

In all of this, what has also helped the stock find some footing is its CDS spread, which has declined by several basis points after it announced a plan to use a $20B equity program to supplement $22.5B in bond issuance for its capital raise in 2026, subsiding fears of a credit downgrade.

Just to reiterate, when it comes to Oracle’s long-term projections, management expects their OCI to accelerate by 77% in FY26 to $18B and scale to $144B by FY30, allowing it to capture roughly 16% of the cloud market, thus putting it at a similar scale to the next closest hyperscaler by FY29.

However, what is important from here onwards is management actually delivering at or above their projections. Any further misses on their top-line growth estimates would lead to serious trouble ahead.

When it comes to profitability, the picture is mixed, as the company has been receiving several downgrades over the past 1-3M period.

Plus, yesterday, the company announced its plans to reduce its workforce by thousands of employees across multiple divisions that could be implemented as soon as this month, which could help stabilize the overall profitability to a certain extent.

The stock is currently trading at just 21x its projected FY26 earnings per share, which is expected to grow 21.77% YoY. The multiple compresses down to just 14.7x when taking the company’s projected FY28 earnings per share into account, which is substantially lower than the 10-year average S&P 500 multiple of 18.8, as well as the price multiples of the four other hyperscalers. This is also unlike a “bubble” environment where you would typically see valuations of companies spike.

So, what is the “right” multiple at which Oracle stock should be trading?

To be honest, it is hard to determine one at the moment given the elevated investor skepticism towards the predictability of its cash flows and ROI on AI capex. I believe the first solid sign of a bottom will be determined when the stock closes above its 200DMA at $220 per share, which represents an upside of 40% from current levels.

Meanwhile, the average price target on Wall Street currently sits at $257 per share, which represents an upside potential of 63% from current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

So, are we finally out of the woods yet?

It's too early to say that.

While investor sentiment on AI capex has been improving over the past week or so after OpenAI’s funding announcement and Nvidia’s Huang defending the current scale of the infrastructure buildout, it is still important to note that the mismatch between investor expectation of AI revenue realization and when it actually materializes may induce several waves of AI bubble fears that can create downward volatility for Oracle’s stock.

In the medium term, however, I believe that if Oracle demonstrates a stronger than expected beat and raise in its Q3 FY26 earnings, it could set the stage for a significant re-rating higher in the stock. As a result, I will maintain my “buy” rating on the stock at current levels.

data.title: Markets Are Underpricing Inflation Risk - The February CPI Preview
data.description:
Consensus Expectations - Near The 2% Target

The U.S. BLS is set to release the February CPI inflation report on March 11th. The consensus expectations are:

  • The core CPI to increase by 0.2% MoM, which would be less than 0.3% MoM in January, and keep the annual core CPI unchanged at 2.5%, and
  • The headline CPI inflation is to increase by 0.2% MoM, unchanged from January; however, due to base effects, this would push the annual CPI down from 2.5% to 2.4%.

Thus, practically, based on these expectations, CPI inflation is very near the Fed's 2% target. Note, CPI inflation is generally around 50 bpt higher than the Fed's preferred PCE inflation measure.

Other Inflation Measures Disagree

However, other official inflation measures show a different picture. The PPI inflation has been actually heating up. Below is the chart of the annual Producer Prices Final Demand Less Foods, Energy, and Trade Services. This measure of PPI is at 3.4%, and it has been rising since June 2025, with the spike in October 2025, which is still in progress. This is likely due to tariffs.

Below is the chart of the Core PCE Price Index Annual Change, and this is the Fed's preferred inflation measure. The core PCE is at 3%, with the spike in December 2025. Note, the core PCE is supposed to be at 2%.

Thus, a cooling CPI inflation is not consistent with other official measures of inflation. Note, CPI inflation is heavily influenced by shelter inflation (rent), which has been falling, and that could be the difference.

Based on the chart below, the national average rent was $1,626 per month, which is an increase of 0.4% over the last year - the lowest increase since 2016. Rents are falling as the vacancy rate is rising, currently at 8.6%, the highest since 2016. This is likely to continue due to slower population growth given the immigration policy, in addition to an increase in multifamily housing construction.

Soft-Data Inflation Indicators

Soft-data inflation indicators are currently mixed.

The ISM Manufacturing Prices Paid Index shows a major spike in inflation for February, and this is consistent with PPI inflation, possibly reflecting the tariffs.

However, the ISM Services Prices Paid Index shows a dip in inflation for February, which actually started in November. Note, the Service Price Index is still well above 50 at 63, which points to high inflation; however, the trend is disinflationary.

The Logistic Managers Index LMI Transportation Prices also shows a spike in transportation inflation for February, and the spike started in October 2025. This is also consistent with a hot PPI inflation.

At the same time, consumer inflation expectations have been falling consistently since May 2025, now at 3.2%, based on Michigan 1-Year Inflation expectations; and this is consistent with a cooling CPI inflation.

Thus, the soft data points to 1) rising inflation in manufacturing, with higher transportation costs, which is consistent with rising PPI inflation, and 2) disinflation in consumer prices due to falling rents, but also possibly due to a weaker labor market, which is consistent with a cooling CPI inflation. In a sense, this is a stagflationary picture.

Market Measures Of Inflation

Investors are generally pricing in well-anchored long-term inflation.

The 5-year/5-year forward inflation expectations are very low at 2.1%. This is a measure of expected inflation over the five-year period that begins five years from today.

The 10Y Breakeven Inflation is at 2.31%, which also reflects a well-anchored long-term inflation expectation. This measure is derived as the difference between the 10Y Treasury nominal yield and the 10Y Treasury TIPS yield (real rate). The measure is well below the critical 2.5% level.

The 5-year Breakeven Inflation is more elevated at 2.5%, but still within the normal range. Note, this market measure of inflation will generally spike first if there is an inflationary shock.

Thus, investors give the Fed vote of confidence, expecting that long-term inflation will remain well-anchored.

Note, the Fed "panics" only when these market measures of inflation start to rise. In fact, the Fed started to hike in 2022 only after the Breakeven Inflation spiked. Thus, it is important to monitor the bond market reaction to the CPI inflation.

The Unfolding Energy Price Shock

The release of CPI inflation will likely be overlooked by investors because there is an unfolding energy price shock with the U.S.-Iran war, and the outcome of this war changes everything.

Currently, WTI Crude Oil (CL1:COM) is trading near $90/barrel. We are in a nightmare scenario where Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz and is bombing the regional energy infrastructure. If this situation persists for a few more weeks, the price of oil could spike well above $100, possibly even above $200 - and currently there is no end in sight.

The spike in oil prices could cause massive inflation over the near term, but also massive deflation if a global recession follows.

The oil futures market expects the war to last for about 4–5 weeks, with prices returning to the mid-70s by June/July.

The Macro Picture

The current spike in oil prices is a part of an unfolding deglobalization trend, which includes global fragmentation into blocks, trade protectionism, imperialism with a hunt for critical commodities, and physical wars. This macro environment will likely create more frequent spikes in various commodity prices, food prices, and supply chain bottlenecks - all pointing to higher long-term inflation.

Thus, investors are underpricing long-term inflation by assuming a well-anchored inflation consistent with the Fed's 2% target.

Other issues to consider include the sustainability of the U.S. Dollar (DXY) as the global reserve currency in this environment and the sustainability of the U.S. fiscal deficit in a situation where global demand for Treasuries could decrease. Both issues point to higher inflation.

Implications

Financial markets have been remarkably resilient and stable despite the turbulent macro environment, and the main reason is stability in the bond market, or specifically due to expectations of well-anchored long-term inflation consistent with the Fed's 2% target.

Thus, the release of the February CPI report will matter only if it moves the bond market.

The Federal Funds futures are currently pricing a policy normalization in 2026, with the Fed cutting one or two times. The 10Y Treasuries are pricing solid growth and anchored inflation. A "hot" CPI could tilt the Federal Funds futures to price one cut, and this could cause some volatility in the stock market.

However, in my opinion, the February CPI release will be overshadowed by the unfolding current energy price spike.

In my opinion, investors are ignoring 1) the inflationary pressures in the PPI and PCE inflation measures, 2) the current energy price shock, and 3) the longer-term inflationary macro picture - thus underpricing inflation risk.

In this situation, 10Y Yields (US10Y) should be higher, closer to 5%, and the Fed should not be thinking about cutting rates.

data.title: Lowe's: Macroeconomic Headwinds Become More And More Concerning (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW) is a home improvement retailer in the United States, providing products for construction, maintenance, repair, or decor.

I started covering the company back in May 2022 with an initial neutral rating, primarily citing macroeconomic headwinds related to the housing market and consumer confidence. Since then almost 4 years have passed, and the macroeconomic environment remains just as challenging as it was back then.

Over the period of my coverage, LOW performed pretty much in line with the broader market (SPY) and the consumer discretionary sector (XLY), at least up to late 2024, when LOW started to slightly underperform.

The aim of my article today is to give an updated view on my previous investment thesis. I will be looking at a set of economic indicators - including consumer confidence and existing home sales - to gauge the macroeconomic landscape, along with the firm's latest quarterly earnings results. Last, but not least, I will also comment on the valuation briefly to see whether the previously established hold rating is still warranted or not.

Earnings

In the most recent quarter, LOW managed to beat analyst estimates on both the top and bottom lines. Revenue came in at $20.6 billion, 11% above last year's figure and $247.5 million above analyst expectations. Adjusted diluted EPS was $1.98, compared to the expectations of $1.94.

While the growth definitely sounds attractive, there are a few key points that I need to highlight.

Comparable sales growth was only a small portion of the total sales growth. Comps increased only 1.3% year-over-year. It means that LOW's sales growth is not driven by organic, same-store revenue growth due to the higher demand for their products, but by new store openings and acquisitions. Two relevant acquisitions from last year were Foundation Building Materials and Artisan Design Group.

When it comes to the balance sheet, we also have to understand that the debt burden of the firm significantly increased compared to the prior year, leading to higher leverage ratios. The acquisitions are primarily to be blamed for the increased debt burden, as they were partially financed by new borrowing.

Higher debt increases the dependency on interest rates and pushes interest expenses higher, leading to a higher risk profile.

The impact of the new debt financing is also clearly visible on the cash flow statement. But when it comes to the cash flow statement, it is actually the return to shareholders, which I would like to highlight.

Returning value to shareholders is always at the forefront of my discussion, as LOW has been a reliable dividend payer for more than 6 decades. Currently the payment is $1.20 per share on a quarterly basis, equivalent to an annual yield of approximately 1.9%. The yield may not be the highest one out there, but it is safe, sustainable, and reliable. The cash generated from operations is more than enough to cover them.

When it comes to the share buybacks, LOW significantly cut back compared to the prior year. Last year, the firm spent more than $4 billion on buying back its stock, which was reduced to only $211 in fiscal 2026. On one hand, I do like share buybacks, and I would like to see them continue. On the other hand, I believe it is a great way to return value to shareholders without any far-reaching consequences, and in certain jurisdictions it could even have a positive tax implication for investors. If they were to increase dividends at a high rate and then cut back when they cannot afford them anymore, it would have significant negative press. Slowing down the share buybacks does not sound as negative, usually.

Now that we discussed results, acquisitions, debt, and return to shareholders, I would like to look a bit forward and form expectations based on the macroeconomic environment.

Let us start with the consumer. Consumer confidence in the United States remains weak. The readings improved somewhat in the prior months, but they remain close to the 10-year lows. Poor sentiment means that people are less certain about their financial health and therefore are usually reluctant to spend higher amounts on durable, non-essential, discretionary goods - like the ones that LOW is selling. It was explicitly mentioned that people are pausing big-ticket upgrades, which is creating significant headwinds for LOW's business, especially for the DIY segment. This is reflected in their cautious guidance as well.

It is not only the consumer that signals a potentially rocky road for LOW ahead. The housing market is also not in the best shape. Existing home sales just plummeted and reached a low, which was last seen in September 2024. This was the biggest fall in almost 4 years.

In my view, as long as the consumer is weak, the housing market is unstable, the mortgage rates are uncertain, and tariff uncertainty persists, the demand for LOW's products is unlikely to increase substantially. For these reasons, I do not like the company fundamentally now, but what I do like is their continued focus on returning value to their shareholders through dividends.

Valuation

To assess the company's fair value, let us look at the following table, which compares LOW's metrics with those of the sector median as well as its own historic metrics. According to most metrics, LOW is selling at a significant premium compared to both the sector and its own history.

I believe that this overvaluation is currently not justified, neither based on the macroeconomic environment nor based on the company fundamentals.

If we narrow down the peer group to a few key players in the industry, the valuation looks somewhat better - relatively. But in absolute terms, I still do not believe that with the growth figures that LOW posted, with its profitability metrics, and with the persisting macroeconomic headwinds, a 20+ P/E ratio is justified. I am bearish not only on LOW itself but on the whole industry in the near term.

For these reasons, I do not think that LOW could be an attractive investment opportunity for value investors.

Conclusions

Despite beating analyst estimates on top and bottom lines, my view on LOW is becoming more bearish due to the persisting macroeconomic headwinds. Consumer confidence and a weak housing market all signal that the demand for LOW's products in the near term is likely to remain pressured.

Most of the growth year-over-year was driven by acquisitions and not organic growth. The acquisitions also created a significant additional debt burden for the company.

From a valuation perspective, I do not find LOW attractive, as the fundamentals and the macroeconomic landscape do not justify such high multiples, in my view.

For these reasons, I downgrade LOW to sell.

data.title: Tango Therapeutics: I'm Not Buying Into Latest Rally, Despite PRMT5 Promise
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of Tango Therapeutics, Inc. (TNGX) surged in value yesterday, rising from ~$12.4, to $17.4 per share, a gain of ~43%, as the Boston, Massachusetts based biotech reported its Q4 and annual earnings, and shared business updates.

After yesterday's gains, Tango stock is now up an impressive 285% since my last note on the company in June last year, in which I awarded stock a Buy rating, and up 31% since my original Buy call in January 2024.

To remind ourselves - as per Tango's 2025 annual report / 10-K submission:

We are currently focused on clinical development of two MTAP-deleted selective PRMT5 inhibitors: vopimetostat (TNG462) for non-CNS cancers, both as a monotherapy and in combination with RAS inhibitors, and TNG456, our next-generation, brain-penetrant PRMT5 inhibitor, for CNS cancers, including glioblastoma (GBM).

There are a couple of other assets in Tango's pipeline, as illustrated in the below slide (taken from a January 2025 investor presentation).

After my first note on Tango, I discussed how positive data from a rival PRMT5-targeting asset developed by Mirati Therapeutics - acquired by Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) in a $5.8bn deal in 2023 - six confirmed objective responses in a study of 18 patients with solid tumors harboring MTAP deletions - had got the market excited about the this drug class, and triggered gains for Tango.

I concluded that:

There may be opportunities to buy Tango stock at much cheaper prices that today's price of $13 per share, as the stock is clearly volatile and influenced by the progress of other drug developers, rightly or wrongly. Inevitably, however, if Tango is on the right track, its valuation ought to soar.

I would urge prospective investors to be patient with Tango, however, and let the four clinical studies progress. With no dilution likely unless candidates return positive, share price needle moving data, I like Tango as a long-term admittedly high risk, biotech play.

Investors patience was certainly tested, as Tango stock sank to a low of $1.1 per share in May 2025. Tango abandoned one of its two PRMT5 candidates, TNG908, after it was able to generate just four partial responses in 70 patients with non-central nervous system ("CNS") tumors, and no responses in brain cancer (glioblastoma patients).

However, I wrote in June 2025 that Tango was teasing a Phase 1/2 study readout for TNG462, and that its stock was on the rise, and also noted that its new candidate:

TNG456 appears to be a next-generation version of TNG908, with management suggesting it is 5x more potent and 3x more selective than its predecessor.

Positive data related to BMY's PRMT5 candidate BMS-986504 also seemed to be lifting shares, and the upside steadily continued after my note.

An overview of how Tango's two PRMT5 work is shown in the slide below:

In October last year, Tango shared its TNG462 - now known as vopimetostat - Phase 1/2b data, from 94 evaluable patients. CEO (at the time) Barbara Weber M.D. was quoted in a press release as follows:

Across the 16 cancer types enrolled in the trial, the ORR is 27% in patients with a median follow-up of 9.4 months, which supports the potential for vopimetostat as the best-in-class PRMT5 inhibitor.

In 2L MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer, the median PFS is 7.2 months and the ORR is 25%, more than double that observed in historical control studies, supporting our decision to initiate a pivotal trial in this patient population in 2026.

With FDA alignment on the go-forward dose of 250 mg QD, we anticipate that this study will enroll rapidly, underscoring the potential for vopimetostat to be the first MTAP-selective PRMT5 inhibitor to market

Tango stock was trading at ~$9 per share at the time, and did not spike on the data release - possibly because the company immediately completed a share offering at $8.66 per share, raising ~$210m, but held its value, and reached ~$13 per share in January this year.

Progress Update - Shares Spike On Series Of Business Updates

Yesterday, Tango reported $62.4m of revenues for 2025, largely related to a development partnership with Gilead Sciences (GILD), the Pharma giant based in California. Tango states in its 10K:

For the full-year 2025, R&D expenses amounted to $132.2m, and total operating expenses were $173.6m, compared to $188m in the prior year. Net loss for the year was $(102m), compared to $(130m) in 2024. A cash position of $343m was also reported.

The reason that the market got excited yesterday relates to a series of positive business updates, however.

Firstly, Tango announced it is on track to initiate "a pivotal study" for vopimetostat monotherapy in second-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer in 2026. Secondly, the company noted "robust enrollment" in its vopimetostat + RAS(ON) inhibitors study in patients with 2L+ MTAP-del, RAS-mutated pancreatic and lung cancer. This is in partnership with two assets, daraxonrasib or zoldonrasib, developed by Revolution Medicines (RVMD), a biotech that was close to being acquired by Pharma giant Merck & Co. last year.

Thirdly, Tango announced that it had entered into a clinical trial collaboration with the San Diego based biotech Erasca (ERAS), to evaluate vopimetostat in a clinical trial combo with ERAS-0015, its pan-RAS molecular glue drug candidate. Erasca's candidate is currently in Phase 1 studies.

Tango also has a new CEO and President, with the company announcing in January the retirement of Barbara Weber, to be replaced by Dr. Malte Peters. The press release noted that:

In addition to his service on the Tango board, he (Dr. Malte) was Chief Research and Development Officer at MorphoSys AG, following his tenure as Chief Development Officer and board member since March 2017, where he oversaw a large development organization and the global regulatory approval of Monjuvi in combination with lenalidomide in diffuse large B cell lymphoma.

Morphosys, whose stock I held at the time, was acquired by Swiss Pharma giant Novartis (NVS) for $2.9bn, in February 2024.

Analysis - Key Catalysts (& Sterner Tests) Await In 2026

Tango has already racked up an accumulated deficit of >$600m since it listed on the Nasdaq via a deal with a Special Purpose Acquisition Company ("SPAC"), Boxer Capital’s BCTG Acquisition Corp in 2021, shares price at $10.

The company says that there are 60k cases of treatable MTAP deleted cancers reported in the U.S. annually, so the future market opportunity is clear, provided Tango can deliver "best-in-class" data in its target indications.

Vopimetostat is taken orally, once daily (a potential competitive advantage) and its twin targets at present are pancreatic and lung cancers. In the pancreatic cohort of its Phase 1/2 study, numbering 64 patients, a median progression free survival of 7.2 months was reported in previously treated patients and an objective response rate ("ORR") of 25%, with an overall ORR of 15% - as we can see below, the PFS is superior to results obtained in historical studies of current standard of care ("SoC") chemo drugs.

The pivotal study in second line pancreatic will evaluate 150 patients treated with vopimetostat against 150 patients treated with SoC chemotherapy, and management says it is aligned with the FDA on study design.

Meanwhile, in 1st line pancreatic, Tango says vopimetostat plus Revolution's zoldonrasib has shown "striking efficacy" in preclinical models, and "encouraging" early activity in a clinical study, which is expected to share data this year. An overview of strategy for this combo is shown below:

There will also be an update provided in 2026 from Tango's 41-patient lung cancer cohort. The company also notes that:

49% ORR and mPFS 9.1 months in histology selective cohort of multiple late line cancer types (excluding sarcoma) provides further evidence of strong vopimetostat activity and additional optionality for development.

Tango is also promising to share TNG456 Phase 1/2 data in MTAP-del solid tumor cancers this year. Finally, while candidate TNG260, focused on CoREST inhibition in STK11-mutant cancers barely garners a mention in the earnings press release, Tango does mention in its 10K filing that:

In November 2025, we announced that patients with checkpoint inhibitor resistant STK11 mutant/KRAS wild-type NSCLC receiving clinically active doses of TNG260 plus pembrolizumab had a mPFS of 29 weeks (n=5), more than double the standard of care PFS of ~10 weeks. 80 mg QD of TNG260 was selected for dose expansion, which is ongoing for patients.

Analysis - What To Make Of Tango In 2026

In summary, there appears to be some genuine promise in the data Tango has shared to date, for vopimetostat, both monotherapy and in combo with Revolution's candidate, and in a range of solid tumors, and to a lesser extent from TNG260, while we await initial data from TNG456.

The data catalysts due 2026 intrigue, although we do not know (to the best of my knowledge) when they may occur. Tango says it is funded into 2028, although I would not be surprised to see the company raise further funding, especially if shares spike based on positive data releases.

The fact that Tango now has a pivotal study ongoing, with a nice, clean design which the FDA is aligned on, is a further positive, although there are caveats to an ongoing Buy thesis.

Data that has been shared to date is immature, and mid-stage and late-stage studies in larger patient sets will provide the acid test of whether Tango genuinely possesses an approvable candidate in vopimetostat - in fact we may have to wait until the pivotal pancreatic data is released before a truly accurate judgement can be made.

Its unclear at this stage how much potential vopimetostat plus zoldonrasib truly has - preclinical data release by any biotech nearly always seems to trumpet "best-in-class" potential, and its even less clear how a combo of vopimetostat plus ERAS-0015 will perform - although personally I am a fan of the molecular glue degrader class of assets, it's hard to point a resounding success for a RAS-pathway targeting asset. Amgen's (AMGN) Lumakras and Bristol-Myers Squibb's Krazati earned ~$550m in the indication of lung cancer last year.

Competition in Tango's field of development is also fierce - as per its 10-K filing:

We are aware that BMS, Amgen, AstraZeneca (AZN), BeOne and Abbisko are in Phase 1/2 clinical trials with MTA-cooperative PRMT5 inhibitor programs, using the same mechanism of action as vopimetostat and TNG456. There may be additional competition from other drug candidates not yet in clinic trials, such as Gilead Sciences and IDEAYA Biosciences (IDYA). Currently, there are no MTA-cooperative PRMT5 inhibitors that are authorized for marketing by any regulatory authority.

Tango's partnership with Gilead has also been scaled back - the company notes in its 10-K filing:

although Gilead provided us with $175.0 million upfront payments and a $20.0 million equity investment in connection with our collaboration, we have since truncated the research term of this collaboration and anticipate that we will need additional funding to advance product candidates prior to receiving any milestones from the collaboration agreement with Gilead.

In conclusion, then, this feels like something of a "don't tell me, show me" year for Tango. There is a strong leadership team in place, few financial concerns, several assets and drug combinations, and multiple data readouts ahead, but target indications are challenging - there is a good reason why second line pancreatic has so few treatment options - most efforts to develop effective drugs in this indication have not succeeded.

Now that Tango's market cap valuation has reached $2.3bn, there is pressure on the company to justify that high (for a biotech that has only just reached the pivotal study stage) valuation with a strong set of data.

In fact, that knock-out data set may not arrive in 2026, as the readouts due will be early stage, and as mentioned, the pivotal pancreatic data could provide the acid test for Tango. As such, although I'm excited about what combo data, and TNG456 data will look like when it is shared this year, I'm downgrading stock to a Hold for the time being.

2026 feels like a year to be patient and watchful with Tango, as opposed to overly optimistic for early-stage data readouts. I would not be surprised if Tango reports one or two study setbacks this year, as all biotechs do from time to time, but remain optimistic for the pivotal study.

data.title: 4 Signs Of Increasing Consumer Stress
data.description:
The U.S. markets have remained quite resilient this week despite the escalating conflict in the Middle East and the associated spike in oil prices. The narrative around the AI revolution remains intact and serves as the key driver of GDP growth in the United States.

Consumer spending is holding up despite lousy consumer sentiment readings, although retail sales were flat in December and down 0.2% month-over-month in January. This is largely due to the bifurcated U.S. economy, which has become known as this K-shaped economy. The top 10% of American households by income now account for roughly half of all U.S. consumer spending. As long as the stock market and real estate markets continue to hold up, this cohort should continue to spend confidently.

However, most of the rest of the consumer base is struggling. And while the AI revolution has dominated the economic and equity headlines in recent years, the U.S. consumer still drives roughly two thirds of GDP in the country. In today's column, I will highlight four concerning signs that the majority of consumers are seeing ever increasing stress.

#1 - Personal Savings Rate

Following the onset of Covid and the pandemic lockdowns, Congress pumped trillions of dollars of additional largesse into the U.S. economy via legislation. A decent chunk of this was via Covid stimulus payments. This bolstered the personal savings rate in the U.S. by a huge measure, as can be seen below.

However, these funds have now been depleted, and the U.S. savings rate stands at a minuscule 3.6%. Higher tax tax refunds this year and recent measures like no tax on the first $25,000 annually of tip income should help over the next couple of quarters, but only marginally.

#2 - Consumer Loan Delinquencies & Sign Of Desperation

Consumer loan delinquencies are rising on student, auto, and credit card debt, as can be seen above. Combined, this represents approximately $4.5 trillion in overall debt. You can see signs of increasing desperation to service consumer debt. A record percentage of Americans are raiding their 401(k) accounts for funds, according to the latest read from Vanguard. This is unfortunately a continuation of a trend that has developed in recent years as inflation has run much hotter than in previous decades.

#3 - Energy Prices Spiking

This is the latest headwind consumers now face. Rising energy costs as the result of the escalating conflict in the Middle East. Already, the average price of a gallon of gasoline has jumped to $3.32 per gallon, its highest level in 18 months. Diesel is over $4.25 a gallon, a level last seen in November 2023. Jet fuel prices have gone up 15% since the bombing over Iran began.

Things could get much worse if most of the oil exports from the Middle East remain locked in. Qatar’s energy minister, as reported by the Financial Times, warned that the conflict may force Gulf energy exporters to shut down production within weeks. Other Gulf states might follow suit.

#4 - Accelerating Jobs Markets Deterioration

With recent revisions, 2025 showed job growth of roughly 15,000 positions a month. A dramatic downturn from recent years following the worst of the Covid pandemic. On Friday, the February BLS jobs report showed a print of a negative 92,000. This was a brutal miss compared to the consensus that was calling for an increase of 55,000. The two prior months were revised down by a total of 86,000 jobs as well. The unemployment rate moved to 4.4% from 4.3% previously.

Despite the huge jobs miss, the yield on the 10-year Treasury did not fall as it usually does in that scenario as oil prices continued to spike on Friday. How much of an impact AI is having on job growth is still speculation at this point, but likely to get more negative in the months ahead. Block (XYZ) announced it was laying off 40% of its entire staff, as those functions will be replaced by AI. Oracle (ORCL) just disclosed new reductions in force to free up investment for its massive AI capex. Overall, information technology lost 11,000 positions in February.

In summary, the surge of tech spending around the buildout of AI infrastructure will continue to be the key economic engine for the U.S. economy, at least through 2026, given capex budgets are committed to at the big hyperscalers.

However, if things continue to deteriorate on the consumer front, will that be enough to deliver decent economic growth in 2026? Will stock market valuations hold if GDP growth projections get ratcheted down in the months ahead?

I have my doubts, as with job growth clearly slowing and now negative so far in 2026, consumer stress continuing to rise, and new inflationary pressures from surging energy prices, the most likely economic scenario ahead feels like stagflation. And that is not an environment equities perform well in historically. And investors should remember the bear markets that occurred due to a rapid surge in oil prices triggered by unexpected events in the Middle East, like in 1973, 1979, and 1990.

data.title: Nvidia: Blowout Q4 Report Aside, Don't Underestimate The Power Of CUDA
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), published in November 2025, I analyzed the company’s Q3 report and explored the key takeaways, with a special focus on the rising competition from the likes of Alphabet (GOOGL) and on a potential partnership between (META) and GOOGL. I had a Strong Buy rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, the stock has gained 1.71%, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained 0.27% during the same period.

NVDA reported its Q4 numbers about two weeks ago and despite a stellar report, after an initial pop during the after-hours, the stock gave all its gains and then some during regular trading hours. This has become a familiar pattern in recent times, which is unfortunate given that the company continues to beat no matter how high the bar is.

In this article, I analyze the company’s Q4 report and explore the key takeaways. I focus predominantly on the surge in supply commitments that was announced by the company and what it means for investors, as well as analyze just how intense the competitive environment has become for NVDA and whether investors have reasons to worry.

A Snapshot of Nvidia’s Q4 Performance

NVDA finished the year with a blockbuster quarterly performance, beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $68.1 billion, up 73% y/y and 20% q/q, comfortably beating analyst estimates by $1.90 billion. Non-GAAP diluted EPS came in at $1.62, up 82% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.08. Data center revenues set a quarterly record, coming in at $62.3 billion, up 75% y/y and 22% q/q, blowing past estimates of $60.4 billion. Q4 gross margins, on a non-GAAP basis, came in at 75.2%, expanding by 160 bps (or 1.6%) sequentially and were in line with analyst estimates. Operation cash flows came in at $36.2 billion, which translates to a y/y growth of 117.8%.

Q1 FY27 guidance also came in very strong. More specifically, Q1 revenues are projected to come in at $78 billion [+/- 2%], which blew past consensus estimates of $72 billion. Non-GAAP gross margins are expected to come in at 75% [+/- 0.5%]. Non-GAAP operating expenses are projected to be approximately $7.5 billion [includes $1.9 billion of stock-based compensation). Finally, non-GAAP tax rates are expected to be in the range between 17% and 19%.

NVDA’s Supply Commitment Surge Lends Credibility to Company’s Growth Story

One of the highlights of NVDA’s Q4 earnings call was when CEO Jensen Huang declared that “the agentic AI inflection point has arrived.” While it’s too early to say whether this point has arrived or not, one thing that is certain is the progress made on agentic AI by the likes of (OPENAI), Anthropic (ANTHRO) and the hyperscalers has only fueled NVDA’s growth story. This can be seen in both the exponential growth in data center revenues in Q4 as well as the increased visibility of strong, sustained demand for its GPUs in FY27.

For instance, as mentioned earlier, data center revenues set a record in Q4 at $62.3 billion. It was the second straight quarter of 20%+ growth and blew past not just analyst estimates, but also the so-called "whisper number."

However, the biggest takeaway for me from NVDA’s Q4 report was that we got further confirmation that the $500 billion in orders throughout 2026, which management had announced a while back, will be exceeded, a point I had touched upon in my previous article, and one that was confirmed by CFO Collette Kress during the Q4 earnings call. More importantly, she also confirmed that the company has both the inventory and supply commitments in place to meet this $500+ billion demand, “including shipments extending into calendar 2027.”This was further confirmed quantitatively when the company announced that the total supply-related commitments hit $95.2 billion by end of Q4 from $50.3 billion at the end of Q3, which lends further proof that the company has the capacity to meet the demand “beyond the next several quarters.”

Overall, the key takeaway that investors should pay more attention to, in my opinion, from NVDA’s Q4 report, is the confirmation of not only having an explosive demand but also the company’s ability to meet the demand in FY27. Having merely demand means nothing in my view. It’s all about how soon the demand can be met. The comment from the CFO, therefore, on the confirmed visibility of meeting the demand, at least in the next twelve months, should be reassuring for investors.

NVDA’s Market Leadership Unlikely To be Threatened Even as Competitors Circle Around

In my last article on NVDA, I argued about how the reaction to a potential META-GOOGL partnership being a serious threat to NVDA was overdone. Q4 offered further confirmation of this. More specifically, NVDA announced that META plans to deploy “millions of Blackwells and Rubin GPUs, NVIDIA CPUs and Spectrum x Ethernet for training and inference.”

Investors should take note that the company has only just shipped its first Vera Rubin samples to customers two weeks back, and the production of these will not commence until the second half of the year. Therefore, for a company like META to already express concrete plans to deploy them for training their AI models is further evidence that NVDA’s market leadership is unlikely to be threatened by the likes of GOOGL or AMD anytime soon. This is because, in my opinion, even if competitors’ products come close from a performance perspective, no competitor has come close to NVDA’s software stack, CUDA.

Take the case of Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), for instance. The company’s Helios [MI455X] offers 432 GB capacity per GPU compared to NVDA’s Very Rubin [R200]’s 288 GB capacity per GPU. Furthermore, on paper, the Helios is projected to have 2.9 exaFLOPS of FP4 reference at rack scale, which makes it faster relative to the R200. Finally, AMD utilizes TSMC's (TSM) 2nm/3nm hybrid chiplet design, making Helios the first GPU to take advantage of the N2 node. As such, there are quite a few advantages that AMD’s Helios has over Vera Rubin, which could make it attractive.

However, when it comes to software stack, AMD is unlikely to replicate the performance of CUDA with its Radeon Open Compute [ROCm] software ecosystem anytime soon. Therein lies the challenge, in my opinion, making it harder for competitors like AMD to displace NVDA’s market leadership. For instance, while the ROCm has made substantial progress in recent months, successfully narrowing the performance gap with CUDA from 40-50% to approximately 10-30%, several challenges still remain, the most important being CUDA’s nearly two-decade lead in optimization and stability.

Moreover, performance-critical GPU workloads remain deeply entrenched in CUDA-specific optimization and while the introduction of AI agents (e.g., Claude Code) has made porting from CUDA to ROCm faster, the ROCm installation still needs more technical expertise (e.g., Linux kernel and driver configuration tweaks) compared to the plug-and-play nature of CUDA.

Finally, when it comes to real-world workload, the predecessor of the MI455X, the MI300X, underperformed the GB200, as NVDA’s throughput advantage increased from 77% at light workloads to more than 105% at high concurrency. This was attributed to the ability of CUDA’s more mature execution stack to continue scaling as workload intensity increased, with the ROCm plateauing earlier. There is a possibility that the MI455X may narrow the gap thanks to its architectural improvements. However, given that CUDA’s advantage is structural and not hardware-dependent, the real-world workload issue for ROCm is unlikely to be resolved by a chip upgrade alone.

The list of CUDA’s advantages doesn’t end there, but it does paint a bigger picture, which is that for all the progress made by the competition when it comes to chip alternatives, when it comes to the software ecosystem, NVDA’s market dominance remains intact. This, in my view, should keep NVDA at the forefront of the AI revolution for the foreseeable future.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Source: Author’s xGBoost Valuation Model.

To calculate the EPS for FY28 [CY 2027], I deployed an ensemble model, which combines a weighted log-linear trend model and a machine learning model [direct 8-horizon XGBoost]. This ensemble model is an upgraded version of the ensemble model that I used to value NVDA in my last article.

The two components can be described as follows:

  • Weighted Log-Linear Trend Model – this model replaces the regime-switching dynamic growth model and helps to extrapolate NVDA’s current growth trajectory rather than decaying it towards a historical average that has become stale due to the ongoing “AI infrastructure regime”, which began back in Q3 2022.
  • Direct 8-Horizon XGBoost Machine Learning Model – this model overcomes the compounding forecast error associated with the previously used recursive random forest ML model. To eliminate the compounding forecast error, a separate model is trained for each of the eight forecast quarters independently. The machine learning model was trained on a panel dataset covering seven semiconductor companies [NVDA, AMD, Broadcom (AVGO), Qualcomm (QCOM), Micron (MU), Marvell (MRVL), TSM], which allowed it to learn the hyper-growth patterns at scale across the broader peer universe.

I undertook a walk-forward validation test for both the models, based on the most recent fourth quarters. The model metrics for each of the models are as follow:

A MAPE less than 5% is considered to be excellent.

A MAPE of 3.08% for the XGBoost implies that it was better suited to determine NVDA’s valuation compared to the recursive random forest that I used in my previous article, which had a MAPE of 10.64%.

The extreme differential [11.8x] in the performance gap between the log-linear trend and the XGBoost models triggered my ensemble model’s dominance gate. The dominance gate is a safeguard that gets triggered when there is an extreme differential and operates as follows: if one model’s MAPE exceeds 2.5x the other, the weaker model is dropped entirely to avoid the addition of noise. Therefore, given the 11.8x differential, the dominance gate was triggered as a result of which, the EPS forecasting relied entirely on the XGBoost model.
