The price target under the Base case scenario suggests an upside of about 50% from current levels. Given that even the price target under the bear case scenario shows a marginal upside from current levels, I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating on the stock.

The negativity surrounding the stock that I mentioned in my last article continues to persist, as evidenced by both a weaker momentum grade [B] on Seeking Alpha’s momentum scale and the disappointing post earnings reaction despite a stellar report. As such, I would recommend a dollar cost-averaging strategy while buying this semiconductor giant.

Risk Factors

In addition to the factors mentioned in my bear case scenario as well as the key risk that I mentioned in my previous article surrounding the potential implosion of the “circular deals,” the other risk factor to my investment thesis remains the potential of the likes of AMD to close the gap on NVDA when it comes to the software ecosystem. AMD, for instance, has made leaps and bounds with respect to Helios, as I mentioned earlier. Should the acceleration persist, then the dominance of CUDA would erode, which would subsequently have an adverse effect on the ability of NVDA to maintain its market leadership.

Concluding Thoughts

It was yet another blowout quarter from NVDA, with the company beating every key measurable street estimate. The data center revenues resumed their accelerating growth and the company confirmed that it would exceed its $500 billion sales estimate and even announced a massive supply commitment to meet the demand to support the confirmation.

There has been a lot of talk recently about how the competitive landscape has become more treacherous for NVDA. While it is true that the number of competitors has increased and NVDA’s market share certainly has gone down, investors often overlook the fact that it’s not only the chips that matter but also the software ecosystem. In that respect, CUDA continues to dominate and while the likes of AMD have certainly closed the gap, the head start that CUDA has had continues to ensure that it’s leadership remains intact. And with more efficient GPUs set to arrive in the form of Vera Rubin, the combination of these and CUDA are likely to ensure that NVDA’s market dominance remains intact for the foreseeable future, as agentic AI reaches an inflection point.

From a valuation perspective, based on the results of the XGBoost machine learning model that I deployed, I see considerable upside at current levels. Therefore, I maintain a Strong Buy rating on the stock and recommend a dollar cost averaging strategy given the relative weak momentum as well as the continued uncertainty surrounding the ongoing geopolitical conflict, which is driving down investor sentiment. There is no debating the fact that competitive intensity has increased for NVDA when it comes to chips. CUDA, however, shields NVDA’s market dominance from this intensity.

data.title: Sensient: Traders Overreacted To The Natural Colorings Thesis
data.description:
I last covered ingredients company Sensient Technologies Corporation (NYSE:SXT) in early 2025. At the time, I credited the firm for its improving operating results but said that it was too early to buy. That initially looked like a poor call, as SXT proceeded to rally sharply later that summer. However, Sensient has now dipped 20% off the 52-week highs. And, in the bigger picture, after 2025's failed breakout, Sensient shares have slid back to around where they were trading five years ago:

So, is it time to buy SXT stock? Here’s why I’m still not convinced that Sensient is a good opportunity just yet.

The Upside Case: Natural Colorings Will Take Off

It's no secret that Dept. of Health and Human Services "HHS" Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has focused on changing America's food landscape. That is part of his "Make America Healthy Again" program. It'd be logical to assume that there would be some federal policy pushing food companies to use healthier or more natural ingredients as a result of RFK. Jr.'s persuasion.

However, interestingly, it appears to be state efforts that are more directly causing action, at least so far. West Virginia, for example, became the first state to ban the sales of foods with synthetic colors, though the law is currently being contested in court. For another example, Texas passed legislation that will force food makers to put warning labels on products that contain certain ingredients, including synthetic colors and titanium dioxide. That law is slated to go into effect in 2027.

While most states have not passed any legislation on these matters, the fact that a couple of states have done so is causing many packaged food companies to change their ingredients regardless. It's inconvenient and more expensive to have to manufacture a certain cereal for 48 states and then have a different set of ingredients for West Virginia and a separate packaging label for Texas. Easier, in many cases, to simply remove the offending ingredient entirely for the whole nation in order to streamline product manufacturing and distribution.

In addition, some private companies are starting to shift their product composition in order to get ahead of these rules. Retailing giant Walmart (WMT) announced that it will eliminate synthetic dyes from all of its private label goods within the next year.

As per Sentient's management, there is about ten times the revenue opportunity for the company in selling coloring made from natural ingredients instead of synthetics. Thus, in theory, this could lead to a windfall for Sensient's natural colors business and lead to a major growth engine for a firm that has otherwise struggled to increase organic sales over the past decade. And to that point, Sensient is investing significantly in new capacity to build out its natural colors business.

A Modest Impact To Operating Results So Far

Sensient stock blasted off in early 2025 as analysts noted that Sensient could become a big winner from the Make America Healthy Again movement. And, given the analysis I presented above, this is a reasonable enough bullish thesis.

However, we're now almost a year removed from the initial excitement around natural colorings. But it hasn't really made any real impact on Sensient's results yet.

Take this quote from the company’s Q4 conference call, for example:

“While some tariff-induced customer supply chain disruptions and unforeseen weather events negatively impacted our fourth quarter results, we still reported 2% local currency revenue growth and flat adjusted local currency operating profit in the fourth quarter.”

I don’t see 2% revenue growth and flat operating profits as something that’s worth SXT stock's 25x forward earnings multiple in the current operating environment.

Investors can reasonably argue that growth is going to accelerate as packaged food companies search for more natural coloring solutions. Perhaps that’s the case. However, the U.S. alone makes up just 5% of the world’s population; more stringent regulations in that market by itself aren’t necessarily a complete game-changer for Sensient. If many other international markets copy the tougher regulations that we're seeing in states like Texas and West Virginia, that would drive a large fundamental shift in the market. But that remains to be seen. It’s also worth remembering that RFK. Jr. is likely to remain in charge of the HHS only through the end of President Trump’s term, meaning that much of the current momentum around natural colorings could subside or even start to reverse in 2029.

Meanwhile, Sensient has its agricultural ingredients business as well, and sales were down outright there in Q4. The company blamed weather issues, such as historic rains, for the setbacks. Regardless, I don’t see products such as dehydrated onions and garlic as ever being especially high-margin or significant growth avenues.

Sensient Stock Verdict

I’m not against the idea of owning Sensient or another flavorings and colorings sort of company. That said, I don’t believe this sort of business is worth an especially high multiple. I see folks compare ingredient companies like Sensient or International Flavors & Fragrances (IFF) to, for example, McCormick (MKC).

However, I think that’s an unfair comparison. McCormick has branded consumer products such as Frank’s RedHot, Cholula, and Old Bay, which have strong loyalty and some degree of pricing power. You could argue that a spice like black pepper or oregano is a commodity in the same way that food colorings are, and that’s probably true to an extent. But I believe that a company that gets much of its business from end consumers can defend higher profit margins and thus a higher valuation multiple than a company like Sensient, which primarily sells to packaged food companies.

In my mind, it simply doesn’t make much sense to pay 25x forward earnings for Sensient, especially in a world where a company like McCormick, which usually trades at a premium valuation, is instead being offered at 21x. To that point, I believe potential Sensient buyers must take into account the fact that the valuations of packaged food companies and consumer staples companies more broadly have taken hits. There’s likely to be less demand for a premium-valued name like Sensient at a time when so many of its peers are selling near 5-to-10-year valuation lows.

With this in mind, it seems rather generous for investors to be assigning a 25x forward P/E multiple to Sensient for potential growth in one of its divisions when even that story really hasn’t played out all that much in the underlying operating results.

To be fair, analysts see Sensient's top-line growth accelerating to 8% in 2026 and 9% in 2027. If that happens, I think shares are reasonably priced at today’s levels. Thus, I rate the stock as a hold today.

But it's worth remembering that Sensient generated $1.5 billion of revenues way back in 2014. It did $1.6 billion last year. That's effectively no growth for more than a decade, certainly not enough to keep up with inflation. For the stock to be reasonably valued at this point, we have to assume that this coloring growth opportunity will transform this stodgy enterprise into a nearly 10% per year growth company going forward. That could well happen, but with the market already pricing that in as the base case, there's not enough potential upside to warrant a buy.

data.title: Potential Tender Offer At MFS High Income Municipal Trust Before Vote
data.description:
I expect to see a tender offer plan announced for MFS High Income Municipal Trust (CXE) in the coming days or weeks. This is among three municipal Closed End Funds (“CEFs”) heavily owned by well-recognized institutional investors that have been subject to shareholder votes needed to approve both a merger and a new advisor.

Earlier this week, the first two (CMU and CXH) announced the boards had approved 50% tender offers at 99% of NAV contingent on shareholder approval. Rationale in each case pointed to encouraging support for the reorganization and providing shareholders with liquidity in advance.

CXE has been the most heavily market discounted of the three funds with high institutional ownership. Its largest institutional shareholders include Saba Capital Management, Almitas Capital, and RiverNorth. In this case, I anticipate all three and other institutional shareholders will ultimately vote for the CXE reorganization if a similar liquidity event exists for CXE.

Background Information

CXE trades around 92 cents per dollar of NAV and pays a municipal income yield of 5.68%. The fund is diversified across states and has been a category underperformer for the last year (3.6% vs 7.3% category NAV) and 10 years (2.25% vs 2.69% category NAV). To date, CXE's bond portfolio has been managed by a well-known US Investment Advisor, MFS Investment Management. MFS was founded in 1924 by George Putnam and is famous for launching the first mutual fund designed for everyday investors, now known as Massachusetts Investors Trust.

CXE has been subject to shareholder Activism by Saba in recent years. Saba filed a 13-D in July of 2023 and has since filed numerous amendments. CXE is also a holding in Saba’s exchange-traded fund (CEFS), which is primarily owned by retail investors.

Last December, MFS and Aberdeen announced planned reorganizations whereby MFS chose to exit its CEF Advisory business in favor of a UK-based advisor named Aberdeen. CMU, CXE, and CXH would be merged into MFS Municipal Income Trust (MFM) with Aberdeen becoming the new investment advisor.

What Are The Risks?

CXE risks merging into MFM, which currently trades at an 8.1% discount to NAV absent any preceding liquidity options if the Board pursues and achieves the vote without the tender offer, my analysis suggests. The eventual distribution rate of MFM has not been finalized. Fund mergers can offer better scale. Still, there would likely be meaningful selling pressure in MFM from those prior shareholders of CXE who did not support the reorganization nor have an opportunity to exit near NAV. To be clear, an excess supply from shareholders who want an exit looks like a far bigger potential problem than any benefits from scale would offset. I expect to lose money on my CXE investment if a tender offer is not announced.

Still, the bigger risk appears to be selling at market now in advance of knowing what liquidity options will exist.

Most Likely Outcome

This looks like a buy because I do not expect the merger and new advisor votes will pass without a tender offer for CXE. The vote typically needed per the Investment Company Act of 1940 amounts to the lesser of 67% or more of shares present at a meeting (if over 50% of outstanding shares are present) or more than 50% of total outstanding shares.
